revision.text:
[[ja:青森県護国神社]] {{draft|qq=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja (Aomori Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Aomori-ken Gokoku Shrine.jpg|280px|Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja]] | caption = Haiden (worship hall) | map_type = Japan Aomori city#Japan Aomori#Japan | coordinates = {{coord|40|36|39.9|N|140|27|56.9|E|region:JP-02|display=inline,title|qq=}} | location = Shimoshiginomachi 1-3, {{ill|Hirosaki|en|Hirosaki|qid=Q194087|lt=Hirosaki City|1=Hirosaki}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}, Japan | deity = 29,184 enshrined heroic spirits (Eirei) from Aomori Prefecture (as of August 2019) | type = Former {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja|1=Gokoku Shrines}}, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} | founded = Meiji 2 (1869), June 6 | festival = April 29 | ceremonies= Eternal Kagura Offering festival on the first Sunday of September | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = Japan | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') }} '''Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (青森縣護国神社, Aomori Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located inside {{ill|Hirosaki Park|ja|弘前公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki Park|qq=}}, on the grounds of {{ill|Hirosaki Castle|en|Hirosaki Castle|qid=Q328582|lt=Hirosaki Castle|1=Hirosaki Castle}} in {{ill|Hirosaki|en|Hirosaki|qid=Q194087|lt=Hirosaki City|1=Hirosaki}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|qid=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture|1=Aomori Prefecture}}. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?|qq=}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|qid=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X|qq=}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|qid=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration|1=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|qid=Q16154911|lt=occupation|1=List of military occupations}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|qid=Q177266|lt=militarist|1=Militarism}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.|qq=}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|qid=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty|1=Treaty of San Francisco}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|qid=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty|1=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. It enshrines 29,184 war dead spirits (eirei) from Aomori Prefecture who died in wars from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} through the conflicts of the modern era (as of August 2019).{{Wayback|url=https://aomorigokoku-housankai.com/about.html |title=About Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja|date=20230402025935|qq=}} Public Interest Incorporated Foundation: Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja Hōsankai == History == * 1869 (Meiji 2), June 6: {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|lt=Tsugaru clan|ja|津軽藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirosaki Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弘前藩|1=Hirosaki Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q903555}} 12th lord {{ill|Tsugaru Tsuguakira|en|Tsugaru Tsuguakira|qid=Q7849981|lt=Tsugaru Tsuguakira|1=Tsugaru Tsuguakira}} held a shōkon (spirit-inviting) ceremony at the south of Hirosaki Castle (Uwano, current-day Hirosaki Apple Park) for 67 Tsugaru domain soldiers who died in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} (including the {{ill|Battle of Hakodate|en|Battle of Hakodate|qid=Q716562|lt=Battle of Hakodate|1=Battle of Hakodate}}).{{Sfn|偕行|2007|p=21|qq=}}{{Sfn|青森縣史 第3巻|1926|p=736|qq=}} * 1870 (Meiji 3): ** March 9: A 14-tsubo shōkondō (spirit hall) was built in Tomita-mura Azatera-zawa.{{Sfn|偕行|2007|p=21|qq=}} *** July 27: Another shōkon ceremony was held for other domain soldiers.{{Sfn|青森縣史 第3巻|1926|p=816|qq=}} *** September 7: Another shōkon ceremony was held to enshrine more spirits.{{Sfn|青森縣史 第3巻|1926|p=821|qq=}}{{Sfn|月刊れぢおん青森|1991|p=41|qq=}} * 1871 (Meiji 4), March 9: A shōkonsha (spirit-inviting shrine) was established, setting May 18 annually for its festival.{{Sfn|青森縣史 第3巻|1926|p=867|qq=}} * 1876 (Meiji 9): ** July 15: Emperor Meiji bestowed an imperial rescript and golden offerings.{{Sfn|青森縣史 第7巻|1926|pp=396-397|qq=}} *** July 23: A special ceremony to further honor the spirits.{{Sfn|青森縣史 第7巻|1926|p=397|qq=}} * 1888 (Meiji 21), December 24: The shrine moved to Kamishiraginomachi No.13 (the current {{ill|Fujita Memorial Garden|ja|藤田記念庭園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujita Memorial Garden|qq=}}).{{Sfn|偕行|2007|p=21|qq=}}{{Sfn|月刊れぢおん青森|1991|p=42|qq=}} {{Sfn|弘前市史|1964|p=590|qq=}} * 1899 (Meiji 32), May: Shōkon ceremony held in the Hirosaki Castle honmaru (main bailey).{{Sfn|偕行|2007|p=21|qq=}}{{Sfn|月刊れぢおん青森|1991|p=42|qq=}} * 1910 (Meiji 43), December 24: As “Hirosaki Shōkonsha,” the shrine moved to its current location inside Hirosaki Park (Hiroyama Castle’s fourth bailey).{{Sfn|弘前市史|1964|p=590|qq=}} * 1936 (Shōwa 11), August 31: Renamed “Aomori Shōkonsha.”{{Sfn|弘前市史|1964|p=590|qq=}} * 1939 (Shōwa 14), April 1: Became an Interior Minister-designated Gokoku Jinja nationwide. The name changed to “Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja.”{{Cite journal |language=ja |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/2960164/1/24 |title=護國神社指定 (Ministry of the Interior Notification No.142, Shōwa 14)|journal=[[Kanpō (government gazette)|官報]] |issue=3670 |date=1939-04-01 |page=46 |doi=10.11501/2960164|qq=}} * 2019 (Reiwa 1), June 5–6: Celebrated the 150th anniversary of the shrine’s founding with a commemorative ceremony.{{Wayback|url=https://www.aomori-izoku.com/gokokushrine.html |title=About Aomori-ken Gokoku Shrine|date=20230323071236|qq=}} Public Interest Incorporated Association Aomori-ken Izoku Rengōkai == Gallery == File:Gate of Aomoriken Gokoku Shrine.JPG|Torii File:Cenotaph of Boshin War at Aomoriken Gokoku Shrine.JPG|Cenotaph for all war dead spirits File:Monument of Kosei Nishidate.JPG|Monument to Nishidate Kosei File:Tanka Monument of Ken Utsuno.JPG|Tanka monument of Utsuno Ken File:Cenotaph for Firefighters at Hirosaki.JPG|Firefighters memorial cenotaph File:Monuments of Yukimoto Kudo and Hiroshi Gatsugita.JPG|Monuments for Kudō Yukimoto (right) and Gatsugita Hiroshi (left) File:Monument of Hyoe Ichinohe.JPG|“Shisei Junchū” (Utmost sincerity and loyalty) monument for Ichinohe Hyoe File:Monument to the Party with Emperor Taisho at Hirosaki.JPG|Monument commemorating a “banquet” during a grand military exercise with Emperor Taishō File:Monument to Minelayer Tsugaru.JPG|Monument to minelayer Tsugaru File:Monument to Monuments Makers at Hirosaki.JPG|“Kenpi Shushōsha” (initial proposer of monuments) monument == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|qid=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|qid=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|qid=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|qid=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|qid=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|qid=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|qid=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * Gokoku Shrines * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|qid=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|qid=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|qid=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|qid=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * Eternal Spring Shrine * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|qid=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|qid=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist|qq=}} === References === {{Reflist|qq=}} == Sources == *{{Cite book |language=ja |editor=Aomori-ken-shi Hensanshitsu |title=Aomori-ken-shi |volume=3 |publisher=Aomori Prefecture |year=1926-05 |doi=10.11501/1020312|qq=}} *{{Cite book |language=ja |editor=Aomori-ken-shi Hensanshitsu |title=Aomori-ken-shi |volume=7 |publisher=Aomori Prefecture |year=1926-05 |doi=10.11501/1020368|qq=}} *{{Cite book |language=ja |editor=Hirosaki-shi Shi Hensanshitsu |title=Hirosaki-shi Shi Meiji・Taishō・Shōwa-hen |publisher=Hirosaki City |date=1964-03-30 |doi=10.11501/3009237|qq=}} *{{Cite journal |language=ja |title= |journal=Gekkan Redion Aomori |issue=April 1991 |publisher=Aomori Chiiki Shakai Kenkyūjo |date=1991-04-10 |doi=10.11501/7953898|qq=}} *{{Cite journal |language=ja |title=Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja |journal=Kaikō |issue=August 2007 |publisher=Kaikōsha |date=2007-08-01 |doi=10.11501/11435762|qq=}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Aomoriken-Gokoku-jinja|qq=}} *[http://www.hirosakipark.jp/505.html Gokoku Shrine - Hirosaki Park] *[https://aomorigokoku-housankai.com/about.html (Archived)] Public Interest Incorporated Foundation Aomori-ken Gokoku Jinja Hōsankai *[https://www.aomori-izoku.com/gokokushrine.html (Archived)] Aomori-ken Izoku Rengōkai * |qq=}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|青森県護国神社|version=100025534|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines|qq=}} {{everybodywiki link|Aomori Gokoku Shrine}} [[ceb:Aomori Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:青森県護国神社]] {{Commons category|Aomoriken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q21652327}}
